tei:respStmt.tei:persName: Timothy L. Jackson, ed. William Drabkin

sdo:itemDesc.dc:title: Handwritten letter from Oppel to Schenker, dated October 19, 1929
sdo:itemDesc.dc:description: Oppel sends Schenker the score of a cantata by Handel, for which he has provided a realization of the bass, and asks him to look over his work.

tei:opener.tei:salute: Mein lieber Herr Professor SchenkerReceipt of this letter is recorded in Schenker’s diary for October 21, 1929: “Von Oppel: Solo-Kantate für Sopran von Händel (1. Buch der Ges. Ausg. No. 14 Gm); bittet um Durchsicht u. Urteil” (“From Oppel, solo cantata for soprano by Handel (Book I of the Collected Edition, No. 14 in G minor); he asks me to go through it and give my judgment”). — The paragraph-break is editorial, prompted by the emdash in the original text. — The letter from Ulrich Oppel OJ 13/10, [92], October 20, 1929, is written on the reverse of this letter and would therefore have been sent in the same envelope.

tei:div.tei:p: hier schicke ich Ihnen meine Bearbeitung einer Solokantate für Sopran u. Bass von Händel; eine Sängerin frug mich nach alter Litteratur u. ich machte mich an die Arbeit. Genehmigen Sie die beiden Arien so? (No 14 im 1. Buch der Solokantate der Gesamtausgabe).The cantata is Dolce mio ben, s’io taccio, HWV 108. Although the cantata was published by Chrysander in volume 50 of the old Collected Edition (Leipzig: Deutsche Händelgesellschaft, 1887), none of the sources attributes it to Handel. The bass part is unfigured, apart from one note in the first recitative. See Timothy L. Jackson, Heinrich Schenker as Composition Teacher: the Schenker-Oppel Exchange, Music Analysis, xx/1 (March 2001), 1–115, where a facsimile of Oppel’s arrangement (now preserved in the Oppel Collection) is given in full, with Schenker’s annotations, on pp. 13–22. An ein paar Stellen habe ich Händels Bass oder Bezifferung geändert, da, wo die blauen Striche darunter u. H am Rande steht, mit Absicht. Mein Prinzip war, die Singstimme nicht zudecken, sondern isoliert zu lassen, u. die Begleitung möglichst selbständig zu gestalten. Die Kantate wird im Nov. gesungen, ich wäre Ihnen daher dankbar, wenn Sie sie bald durchsehen würden; ich weiss zwar, dass das bei Ihrer vielen Arbeit ein unbescheidener Wunsch ist, aber anderseits gilt mir kein Urteil höher als das Ihre. –
Den Jungen gehts gut, mir soweit auch, und das gleiche hoffe ich von Ihrer lieben Frau und Ihnen selbst. Schicken Sie mir das Stück dann nach Leipzig zurück, bitte, Grassistr 8, Landeskonservatorium.

I am sending you here my realization of a solo cantata by Handel for soprano and figured bass; a singer had asked me for some older literature and I set to work; do you approve of the two arias as I have done them? (No. 14 in the first book of solo cantatas in the Collected Works.) In a few places I have altered Handel's bass, or his bass figuration; these places are marked with blue lines underneath and “H” in the margin. My principle was not to cover over the vocal line, but rather to leave it isolated, and to shape the accompaniment so as to be as independent as possible. The cantatas will be sung in November; therefore, I would be grateful to you if you could look through them soon; I know that, in light of your heavy workload, this is a presumptuous request, but on the other hand no critique is more valuable to me than yours. –
The boys are doing well, and I am so far as well, and I hope the same for your dear wife and you yourself. Please send the piece back to me in Leipzig, Grassistrasse 8, State Conservatory.

tei:closer.tei:salute: to the two of you from all of us
